case_name: Payas v. Adventist Health Sys./Sunbelt, Inc.

summary.brief: Payas v. Adventist Health Sys./Sunbelt, Inc.. Medical malpractice case involving cardiology.

evidence_index.text: In July 2009, Dr. Parra-Davila performed a paraesophageal hernia repair surgery on Mr. Galarza using a surgical robot.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: During the surgery, part of the surgical robot detached and became embedded in Mr. Galarza's esophagus.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: A second surgery was performed by a different surgeon at Tampa General Hospital in July 2012 to determine the cause of his worsening symptoms, but the surgery was unsuccessful.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In January 2013, a third, exploratory surgery was performed at Tampa General Hospital. During that surgery, Mr. Galarza's vena cava vein was ruptured, causing considerable blood loss, cardiac arrest, and ultimately his death.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: An autopsy revealed the presence of a thin, coiled band encircling the gastroesophageal junction.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We agree and reverse the decision of the trial court.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Counts IV and V sufficiently charged the vicarious liability of Celebration Health, and thus the trial court erred in dismissing counts IV and V.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Payas's allegations were sufficient to state a claim that Celebration Health had a nondelegable duty when it provided the robotic surgery to Mr. Galarza, and the trial court erred in dismissing count I with prejudice.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: We express no opinion on whether Payas will be able to prove this claim, or any other claim, against Celebration Health.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Accordingly, we reverse the order dismissing counts I, II, IV, and V with prejudice and remand for further proceedings consistent with this opinion.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Mr. Galarza presented for surgical repair of a paraesophageal hernia. The surgery was performed using a surgical robot.

requestables.name: CT scan
requestables.description: CT scan performed prior to second surgery

reveal.clinical_significance: Indicated presence of a foreign object potentially related to surgical complications
reveal.decision_impact: Should have prompted further investigation and patient notification

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents for elective paraesophageal hernia repair

timeline_phases.name: Surgical Procedure
timeline_phases.description: Paraesophageal hernia repair using surgical robot

key_events.event: Surgical robot part detaches and embeds in esophagus

timeline_phases.name: Post-Surgical Complications
timeline_phases.description: Patient experiences complications leading to further surgeries

key_events.event: Third exploratory surgery resulting in vena cava rupture

decision_points.clinical_context: During the paraesophageal hernia repair surgery
decision_points.prompt: What should be done to ensure no foreign objects remain in the patient post-surgery?

options.description: Conduct a thorough count and inspection of all surgical instruments and materials
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard practice to prevent retained foreign objects

options.description: Proceed without verifying all instruments and materials are accounted for
options.clinical_reasoning: Neglecting this step increases risk of retained foreign objects

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant did not ensure all surgical materials were accounted for

expected_action_court.description: Ensure all surgical materials are accounted for

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to account for all surgical materials led to retained foreign object
explanation.what_should_have_happened: A thorough count and inspection should have been conducted
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by surgical protocols

patient_outcome.description: Patient died following complications from multiple surgeries

key_findings: Reversal of trial court's dismissal of claims
Sufficient allegations for vicarious liability and negligence

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to account for surgical materials
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Proper accounting could have prevented retained foreign object

factual_timeline.event: Second surgery performed at Tampa General Hospital
